text: The patient is positioned in a right side up modified flank position. A 12mm assistant umbilical port is placed first, followed by four robotic ports aligned in a straight line along the midclavicular paramedian lines, each spaced eight centimeters apart.

The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava after mobilizing the ascending colon from Girota's fascia and medially cocorizing the duodenum. A laparoscopic intraoperative ultrasound probe surveys the IVC, revealing thrombus in the suprarenal IVC. The right renal artery is identified in the inter-aorta cava location and divided using non-absorbable clips to avoid manipulation of the right renal vein and tumor thrombus.

The surgeon applies bulldog vascular clamps to the contralateral renal vein, infrarenal IVC, and suprarenal IVC. A cavotomy is made along the anterior surface of the IVC, revealing tumor invasion into the IVC wall and lumen of the posterior lumbar branches. A 5.5 cm segment of the IVC is resected due to clot burden and tumor invasion. A 6 cm tubularized synthetic polyester graft is anastomosed to the cephalad portion of the native IVC using PTFE non-absorbable monofilament suture in a bidirectional fashion.

The surgeon anastomoses the graft to the caudal portion of the native inferior vena cava (IVC) using IV-O PTFE non-absorbable monofilament suture in a bidirectional fashion. The infrarenal bulldog clamp is briefly opened to flush out air and clots from the reconstructed area before being reapplied. The anastomosis is completed, and the bulldog clamps are sequentially removed, showing no evidence of graft leakage. An intraoperative ultrasound confirms the patency of the graft and the superior and inferior portions of the vena cava.

The inferior vena cava (IVC) is cross-clamped, and a longitudinal cavotomy is made along its anterior surface. The IVC filter is visibly embedded within the vena cava, with broken prongs intruding into the vessel wall. The surgeon begins to remove the filter piece by piece.

After removing all visible pieces of the IVC filter, the surgeon resects the desmoplastic reaction along the anterior wall of the vena cava to eliminate excess calcifications. The cavotomy measures eight centimeters. A synthetic patch graft is anastomosed to the defect using 5-0 PTFE non-absorbable monofilament suture in a running fashion. The medial aspect is anastomosed first, followed by the lateral aspect, ensuring a watertight closure.
